summary:
The earliest known maps in the world are clay tablets from Mesopotamia, dating to approximately 2300–2500 BCE, but precursors to formal cartography appear in the Chalcolithic period. A clay tablet from Ga-Sur (Nuzi) in northern Iraq, dating to around 2300 BCE, depicts a river valley with settlements and hills marked by conventional signs and is often cited as among the oldest surviving maps. Earlier schematic representations of space — land surveys, property boundary markers, and architectural plans drawn on clay tablets — appear in the [[event:1617|Uruk period]] administrative record from the late fourth millennium BCE.

The emergence of map-making is closely tied to the administrative needs of the first cities: surveying agricultural land for taxation, planning irrigation systems, and recording property rights all required graphic representations of space. The [[event:1619|cuneiform]] writing system developed in tandem with these spatial recording practices, and many early clay tablets combine textual and graphic information. These early maps reflect a fundamentally practical cartographic tradition that predates the theoretical and cosmographic mapping traditions of later antiquity by many centuries.
